title: What Is a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
When a construction or development project encounters a site identified during initial survey work, federal and state regulations may require a Phase II archaeological evaluation before work can continue. Understanding this process helps project managers, federal agencies, and private developers plan timelines and budgets accurately.

Quick Answer: A Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted investigation that determines whether a previously identified archaeological site meets the criteria for listing on the National Register of Historic Places. It involves controlled test excavations, laboratory analysis, and a formal report submitted to the State Historic Preservation Office for eligibility review.

After a Phase I survey identifies one or more archaeological sites within a project area, the next step is determining whether those sites are historically significant enough to warrant protection. This is the purpose of a Phase II archaeological evaluation — to collect the data needed to make a National Register of Historic Places (NRHP) eligibility determination under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA).

For federal undertakings or projects with a federal nexus, this determination is required before permits are issued or federal funds released. State DOTs, utility companies, and private developers working on federally assisted projects encounter this requirement regularly.

What Does a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Involve?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation focuses on gathering enough data to assess a site's integrity, research potential, and significance. It goes beyond the shovel test pits used in Phase I work, targeting specific site areas with systematic excavation units designed to answer defined research questions.

Archaeologists typically open 1x1 meter or 1x2 meter units at regular intervals across the site. Each unit is excavated by hand in natural or arbitrary levels, with all sediment screened through mesh or processed by water flotation to recover small artifacts, botanical remains, and faunal material.

Laboratory analysis follows fieldwork. Recovered artifacts are cleaned, catalogued, and analyzed for chronological and cultural affiliation. Radiocarbon dating, soil micromorphology, or other specialized studies may be conducted depending on site type. Findings are compiled into a formal evaluation report presenting an eligibility recommendation to the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO).

When Is a Phase II Evaluation Required?

A Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site with sufficient artifact density, feature presence, or contextual integrity to warrant further study. Not every site found in Phase I requires Phase II work — only those considered potentially eligible for the NRHP.

Federal undertakings governed by Section 106 of the NHPA must address all identified archaeological sites before federal permits, licenses, or funding are approved. This includes projects funded by FHWA, HUD, the Army Corps of Engineers, and other federal agencies.

State DOTs routinely require Phase II evaluations for transportation improvement projects crossing areas with known cultural resources. Private developers whose projects require federal permits — such as Section 404 wetland permits — face the same requirements. The lead federal agency and the SHPO typically agree on scope through scoping consultation before fieldwork begins.

How Is NRHP Eligibility Determined?

To be eligible for the National Register of Historic Places, an archaeological site must meet at least one of four criteria (A through D) and retain sufficient integrity. Most archaeological sites are evaluated under Criterion D — the potential to yield information important to history or prehistory.

Archaeologists assess whether the site can answer specific research questions meaningful within the regional context. A site containing well-preserved stratified deposits, datable features such as hearths or storage pits, or a diverse artifact assemblage is more likely to meet Criterion D than a scatter of redeposited surface material.

Integrity is equally critical. A site must retain the physical characteristics that make it significant — including location, setting, and materials. Extensive disturbance from plowing, grading, or erosion can compromise integrity even when artifact counts are high. The Phase II report presents all findings with supporting data, photographs, and maps so the SHPO can issue a formal concurrence decision.

How Long Does a Phase II Evaluation Take?

The timeline for a Phase II archaeological evaluation depends on site size, artifact density, the scope of specialized analyses required, and agency review schedules. Small evaluations may be completed in two to four months. Larger or more complex sites can take six months to a year or longer.

Fieldwork typically takes one to three weeks, depending on the number of excavation units in the approved research design. Laboratory processing and analysis often require more time than fieldwork — particularly when water flotation, faunal identification, or radiocarbon dating is involved.

SHPO review periods vary by state and must be built into the project schedule from the start. Working with an experienced CRM firm that maintains strong relationships with state and federal agencies helps streamline review and keeps projects on track.

What Happens After the Evaluation?

If the Phase II evaluation determines that a site is not eligible for the NRHP, the federal agency issues a No Historic Properties Affected finding and the project may proceed. This is the most common outcome for sites with limited integrity or low research potential.

If the site is determined eligible, Section 106 consultation continues. The project sponsor must then either avoid the site through redesign, or proceed with a Phase III data recovery excavation — a more intensive investigation that recovers the site's significant information before it is impacted. Using GIS and remote sensing tools early in project planning can help identify sensitive areas and avoid triggering Phase II requirements altogether.

When avoidance is not feasible, a Memorandum of Agreement documents the mitigation measures agreed upon by all consulting parties, including the SHPO, the lead federal agency, and tribal nations when applicable. The project documentation then becomes part of the permanent cultural resources record.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between Phase I and Phase II archaeological surveys?

A Phase I survey identifies and documents archaeological sites within a project area using shovel test pits and pedestrian survey. A Phase II evaluation follows up on sites found in Phase I, using systematic test excavation units to determine whether those sites are eligible for listing on the National Register of Historic Places.

How much does a Phase II archaeological evaluation cost?

Costs vary based on site size, number of excavation units, required laboratory analyses, and report complexity. Small site evaluations may range from $15,000 to $50,000 or more; larger or complex sites cost significantly more. A qualified CRM firm can provide a project-specific scope and cost estimate after reviewing available site data and project plans.

What makes an archaeological site eligible for the National Register?

A site must meet one of four NRHP criteria (A–D) and retain sufficient integrity. Most archaeological sites are evaluated under Criterion D — the potential to yield important historical or prehistoric information. Integrity of location, association, and materials is assessed alongside the site's ability to address specific research questions.

Can a Phase II evaluation stop a construction project?

A Phase II evaluation can delay a project if a site is found eligible for the NRHP, triggering further Section 106 consultation. It does not automatically stop construction. Options include project redesign to avoid the site, a Phase III data recovery excavation, or a Memorandum of Agreement documenting agreed-upon mitigation measures acceptable to all parties.

Plan Your Project With Confidence

Understanding the Phase II archaeological evaluation process helps project sponsors avoid costly surprises and keep federal compliance on schedule. Early coordination with a qualified CRM firm is the most effective way to manage cultural resource risk.

Our RPA-certified archaeologists bring regional expertise and strong agency relationships to every project, from initial scoping through final SHPO concurrence.
